section.title: Greeting
section.sectionRationale: The letter opens with an unusually long greeting for so short a book. Before a single instruction is given, Paul compresses the whole gospel into four verses: his own commission, the faith and knowledge of God's elect, the hope of eternal life promised by a God who cannot lie, and grace and peace from the Father and Christ our Savior.
section.thesis: The ministry of the truth rests on the unbreakable promise of the God who cannot lie, aiming at a godliness that flows from the knowledge of the truth and the sure hope of eternal life in Christ our Savior.
section.frameNote: Two movements: Paul's expansive self-introduction, tying his apostleship to faith, truth, godliness, and eternal hope; then the address to Titus with the apostolic blessing of grace and peace.

verses.text: Paul, a {{doulos|servant}} of God and an apostle of Jesus Christ for the faith of God’s elect and their {{epignosis|knowledge of the truth}} that leads to {{eusebeia|godliness}},
in the hope of eternal life, which God, who {{apseudes|cannot lie}}, promised before time began.
In His own time He has made His word evident in the proclamation entrusted to me by the command of God our {{soter|Savior}}.
To Titus, my true child in our common faith: Grace and peace from God the Father and Christ Jesus our {{soter|Savior}}.

doulos.gloss: A slave or bondservant, one wholly owned by another. Paul opens not with rank but with belonging: he is God's slave before he is Christ's apostle. The title marks total submission to a Master, yet for Paul it is an honour, echoing the servants of the LORD in the Old Testament like Moses and the prophets.

epignosis.gloss: Full, deep, personal knowledge, stronger than bare information. It is a recurring aim in the Pastoral Epistles: not curiosity but a settled recognition of the truth that grips the whole person. Here it is the knowledge of the truth that produces godliness, so that right belief and right living are bound together.

eusebeia.gloss: Godliness, true devotion to God that shapes conduct, a favourite word of the Pastoral Epistles. It is not mere religion but reverence that shows in daily life. In Titus the whole point of sound doctrine is a godliness the watching world can see, so truth and holiness are never separated.

apseudes.gloss: Free from falsehood, unable to lie, entirely truthful. Used only here in the New Testament, it grounds the believer's hope not in wishful thinking but in the character of God Himself. On Crete, an island famed for lying, Paul anchors everything on the one God who cannot deceive.

soter.gloss: Saviour, one who rescues and preserves. Titus uses the title six times, applied both to God the Father and to Christ Jesus, weaving them together. In a culture where Caesar was hailed as saviour, Paul insists that God our Saviour, made known in Jesus, is the true source of rescue.

basic.context: This is the opening of a letter from the apostle Paul to Titus, his trusted younger coworker, whom he had left on the island of Crete to organise the young churches there. Instead of a quick hello, Paul packs the whole gospel into his greeting, reminding Titus and the church who God is and what the message is before he gives a single instruction.
basic.hermeneutics: Paul's introductions are never throwaway lines. Here he lays out the foundations of the whole letter: God's truth, the goal of godly living, the certain hope of eternal life, and grace from the Father and the Son. Everything Titus is told to do later grows out of these opening truths.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul calls himself a doulos, a slave, of God. He speaks of the epignosis, the deep knowledge, of the truth that produces eusebeia, godliness. He anchors hope in the God who is apseudes, who cannot lie. And he twice calls God and Jesus our soter, our Saviour, a title he loves in this letter.
basic.typology: Paul stands in the line of the servants of the LORD, like Moses and the prophets, entrusted with God's word. The promise made before time began and now revealed in the gospel shows the whole Old Testament pointing forward to what God has done in Christ.
basic.christ: The greeting moves from God our Saviour to Christ Jesus our Saviour, holding Father and Son together as the one source of salvation. The eternal life promised before time began is made real and preached in Jesus, so that the gospel Titus carries is the fulfilment of God's ancient plan.
basic.god: We meet a God who elects a people, who cannot lie, who promised eternal life before time began, and who reveals His word at just the right time. He is called our Saviour, a God whose very nature is to rescue.
basic.crossReferences: Second Timothy 1 speaks of the same grace given before time began and now revealed. Romans 1 shows Paul introducing himself and the gospel in a similar way. And Ephesians 1 unfolds the election, before the world was made, that Paul mentions here.
basic.oneStory: The letter begins where the whole Bible begins, with a God who plans salvation before time, keeps His promise because He cannot lie, and reveals it in Christ. Titus is sent to plant that gospel in ordinary lives on a difficult island, one more link in God's long work of saving a people for Himself.

context.literary: An unusually full salutation for such a brief letter, front-loading the letter's key themes: truth, godliness, hope, and salvation. It follows the ancient letter form, sender, recipient, greeting, but expands each part into a dense summary of the gospel that frames all that follows.
context.historical: Paul writes, most likely in the mid-60s between his Roman imprisonments, to Titus, a Gentile convert and proven coworker, left on Crete to bring order to newly planted churches. Crete had a notorious reputation in the ancient world, which sharpens Paul's stress on truth from a God who cannot lie.
context.canonical: One of the three Pastoral Epistles, alongside 1 and 2 Timothy, concerned with church order, sound doctrine, and godly leadership. The greeting sets Titus within the sweep of God's redemptive plan, from a promise before time to its proclamation now.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish Paul's God-given authority and the truth of the gospel at the very start, so that everything Titus is charged to teach and do rests on a sure foundation rather than on human opinion.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is largely descriptive of the gospel and Paul's calling, yet it carries an abiding prescription for all ministry: sound teaching aims at godliness, and Christian hope rests on the trustworthiness of God, not on results or personality.

debates.question: What does Paul mean by knowledge of the truth that leads to godliness?

views.label: Knowledge produces holy living
views.case: True knowledge of the gospel necessarily bears fruit in godliness; doctrine and life cannot be split, which is the whole burden of Titus.

views.label: Godliness is the aim, not a mere byproduct
views.case: Paul frames the truth as being for the sake of godliness, so that right belief is never an end in itself but is ordered toward a transformed life.

debates.bearing: Either way, Titus refuses to separate creed from conduct: sound doctrine that leaves life unchanged is not the knowledge Paul means.

study.originalLanguages: Paul introduces himself as a doulos, a slave owned by God, before naming himself an apostle, so belonging comes before office. His ministry serves the faith and the epignosis, the full knowledge, of the truth, and that truth is ordered toward eusebeia, godliness, a favourite Pastorals word for reverence that shows in life. The hope of eternal life rests on the God who is apseudes, incapable of lying, a pointed word on an island proverbial for deceit. Twice Paul names our soter, our Saviour: first God the Father, then Christ Jesus, binding the two together as the single fountain of salvation.

typology.type: Paul as servant of God entrusted with His word
typology.fulfillment: He stands in the line of Moses and the prophets, servants of the LORD, now carrying the gospel of the risen Christ to the nations.

typology.type: Eternal life promised before time began
typology.fulfillment: The ancient promise, hidden in God's purpose, is now revealed and proclaimed in Jesus at the proper time.

study.christ: The greeting holds Father and Son together as our Saviour, verse 3 naming God our Saviour and verse 4 naming Christ Jesus our Saviour. The eternal life God promised before time began is grounded in Christ and made known through the gospel Paul preaches. Jesus is the one in whom God's timeless plan of salvation steps into history, so that the truth Titus teaches is not a new idea but the appearing of what God intended from the beginning.
study.god: God is sovereign, choosing a people, His elect, before the world began. He is utterly truthful, one who cannot lie, so His promises are unshakable. He is purposeful, revealing His word at the proper time, and He is a Saviour by nature, a title Paul gives Him again and again in this letter. This is the God whose character makes Christian hope secure.

crossReferences.note: Grace given in Christ before time began, now revealed through the appearing of our Saviour, closely parallels Titus 1:2-3.
God chose His people in Christ before the foundation of the world, the election Paul assumes here.
God is not a man, that He should lie, the Old Testament root of Paul's God who cannot lie.
Paul's parallel greeting, servant and apostle set apart for the gospel that calls the nations to the obedience of faith.

study.oneStory: Titus opens on the same note as the whole Bible: a God who purposed salvation before time, who cannot lie, and who reveals His word in Christ at the right moment. Paul, a servant carrying that word, is a link in the long chain of God's saving work, and Titus is sent to root the gospel in the everyday life of a struggling church. The letter's first breath is the promise that runs from eternity to the cross and out to the nations.

sermon.title: Titus: An Introduction
sermon.why: Begg opens his expository series A Study in Titus with these first four verses, setting the letter in its Cretan context and drawing out Paul's gospel-saturated greeting: the truth that leads to godliness, the God who cannot lie, and grace from God our Saviour. A clear, orthodox handling of this movement.

quiz.q: How does Paul describe himself at the very start of the letter?

options.text: As a servant, or slave, of God and an apostle of Jesus Christ.
options.note: Paul names his belonging to God before his office, verse 1.

options.text: As the founder and head of the church in Crete.
options.note: Paul planted churches, but he introduces himself as God's servant and apostle, not their head.

options.text: As a free citizen writing on his own authority.
options.note: His authority is derived, by the command of God, not his own.

options.text: As a stranger unknown to Titus.
options.note: He calls Titus his true child in a common faith; they are close coworkers.

quiz.q: What is the goal of the knowledge of the truth that Paul serves?

options.text: Godliness, a life of reverence that flows from knowing the truth.
options.note: The truth leads to godliness, binding doctrine and life together, verse 1.

options.text: Winning arguments against false teachers.
options.note: Sound doctrine does refute error later, but its aim here is godly living.

options.text: Gaining secret or advanced spiritual information.
options.note: Paul means truth that transforms life, not hidden knowledge.

options.text: Personal comfort with no change in behaviour.
options.note: The whole point is a changed, godly life.

quiz.q: On what does Paul ground the hope of eternal life?

options.text: On God, who cannot lie, and who promised it before time began.
options.note: Hope rests on God's truthful character and ancient promise, verse 2.

options.text: On the sincerity of the believer's feelings.
options.note: Assurance is real, but its foundation is God's promise, not our feelings.

options.text: On the good reputation of the Cretan churches.
options.note: Crete was famed for the opposite; hope rests on God, not on them.

options.text: On keeping the law perfectly.
options.note: The hope is promised by grace, grounded in God who cannot lie.

quiz.q: Who is called Saviour in this greeting?

options.text: Both God the Father and Christ Jesus, held together as our Saviour.
options.note: Verse 3 names God our Saviour and verse 4 Christ Jesus our Saviour.

options.text: Only Christ Jesus.
options.note: Christ is our Saviour, but Paul also calls the Father our Saviour here.

options.text: Only God the Father.
options.note: The Father is called Saviour, but so is Christ Jesus in the next verse.

options.text: Titus, who saves the church by his leadership.
options.note: Salvation belongs to God and Christ, not to Titus.

quiz.q: Why does Paul pack so much gospel into a simple greeting?

options.text: To lay the foundation of truth on which the whole letter's instructions will rest.
options.note: The opening frames everything that follows in God's saving plan.

options.text: Simply to fill space before the real content.
options.note: It is far from filler; it is the theological foundation of the letter.

options.text: To show off his learning to Titus.
options.note: Paul aims to establish gospel truth, not to impress.

options.text: Because he had nothing practical to say.
options.note: The letter is intensely practical; the greeting grounds that practice in the gospel.

questions: Paul calls himself a servant of God before he claims any title. How would seeing yourself first as one who belongs to God reshape the way you view your work and your worth?
Paul says the knowledge of the truth leads to godliness. Where in your life is there a gap between what you believe and how you actually live?
Our hope rests on a God who cannot lie. When your feelings or circumstances make God's promises feel uncertain, how does His truthful character steady you?
God promised eternal life before time began and revealed it at just the right moment. How does knowing your salvation was planned in eternity change how you face today?
Twice God is called our Saviour. What are you tempted to look to as a saviour instead, and what would it mean to rest in the one true Saviour this week?

translationNotes.note: The phrase can be read as the faith of God's elect and their knowledge of the truth, or as Paul's apostleship being for the sake of that faith and knowledge; the sense is that his ministry serves God's chosen people toward saving truth.
Before time began renders a Greek phrase literally meaning before times eternal, as the BSB footnote notes, pointing to God's plan in eternity past.
God our Savior in verse 3 refers to the Father, while verse 4 gives the same title to Christ Jesus, a deliberate pairing that runs through the letter.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Titus 1:1-4. No em or en dashes appear in this passage.
